title: Imbalanced Learning
description: Techniques for learning from imbalanced data: resampling, cost-sensitive learning, threshold moving, ensemble methods, evaluation metrics, and anomaly detection.
details: Real-world classification is almost always imbalanced — fraud (0.1% of transactions), rare disease diagnosis, network intrusion detection. This file covers the complete toolkit: SMOTE and variants (Borderline-SMOTE, ADASYN, SMOTE-NC), cost-sensitive learning (class weights, sample weights), threshold moving (why it often beats resampling), ensemble methods for imbalance (balanced RF, EasyEnsemble, RUSBoost), proper evaluation (precision-recall curves, AUC-PR, lift charts), and anomaly detection (Isolation Forest, LOF, One-Class SVM, autoencoder-based detection). Every FAANG ML interview at senior level includes at least one imbalanced learning question.

sections.title: The Imbalanced Learning Problem
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: When 99% of your data is class A and 1% is class B, a model that always predicts A achieves 99% accuracy — but is completely useless. Standard ML algorithms assume balanced classes and optimize for overall accuracy, ignoring the rare but important class.

Why imbalanced data is hard:

  • Signal sparsity: The minority class has very few examples — the model can't learn its decision boundary well.
  • Noisy gradients: SGD updates are dominated by the majority class. The minority class contributes little to the loss.
  • Evaluation illusion: 99% accuracy is meaningless. You need precision, recall, F1, and AUC-PR to see the true picture.
  • Threshold mismatch: Models are trained to minimize loss at the default threshold of 0.5, but the optimal threshold for imbalanced data is often much lower (e.g., 0.1).

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Fraud detection: 10K transactions, 100 fraudulent (1%). Model predicts all non-fraud: accuracy = 99%, but recall = 0%, precision = undefined. Useless.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import numpy as np

# === The accuracy trap ===
y_true = np.array([0] * 9900 + [1] * 100)
y_pred_all_zero = np.zeros(10000)  # predict majority always

print(f'Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred_all_zero):.3f}')  # 0.990
print(f'Recall:   {recall_score(y_true, y_pred_all_zero):.3f}')   # 0.000
print(f'Precision:{precision_score(y_true, y_pred_all_zero):.3f}')  # 0.000
print(f'F1:       {f1_score(y_true, y_pred_all_zero):.3f}')       # 0.000

# === Imbalance ratio ===
ratio = y_train.sum() / len(y_train)
print(f'Positive ratio: {ratio:.4f} ({1/ratio:.0f}:1 imbalance)')

# === Detection of imbalance severity ===
# Mild: 10:1 (90% majority)
# Moderate: 100:1 (99% majority)
# Severe: 1000:1 (99.9% majority)
# Extreme: 10000:1 (99.99% majority — fraud, rare disease)

sections.title: Resampling: SMOTE & Variants
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Balance the dataset by adding synthetic minority samples (oversampling) or removing majority samples (undersampling). SMOTE creates interpolated synthetic samples rather than duplicating existing ones.

Methods:

  • Random Undersampling: Randomly remove majority class samples. Simple, reduces training time, but discards potentially useful data. Can lose decision boundary information.
  • Random Oversampling: Duplicate minority class samples. Simple but can cause overfitting (model memorizes exact copies). No information loss.
  • SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique): For each minority sample, find k nearest neighbors (typically k=5). Pick a random neighbor. Create a synthetic sample at a random point on the line between the two. This creates plausible new samples, not just copies.
  • SMOTE Variants:
    • Borderline-SMOTE: Only oversample minority samples that are near the decision boundary (hard to classify). More focused, often better results.
    • ADASYN: Adaptive synthetic sampling — generates more samples for minority samples that are harder to learn (more neighbors from majority class).
    • SMOTE-NC: SMOTE for nominal + continuous features. Handles mixed data types.
  • Combination (SMOTE-Tomek): SMOTE + remove Tomek links (pairs of opposite-class nearest neighbors). Creates cleaner class boundaries.

[Practical Workflow] Critical rule: Apply SMOTE AFTER train/test split, NEVER before. If you SMOTE before splitting, synthetic minority samples contaminate both train and test — you evaluate on data you created, giving inflated performance. ALWAYS: split → SMOTE only on training data.
sections.codeBlock:
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE, ADASYN, BorderlineSMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler, TomekLinks
from imblearn.combine import SMOTETomek
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import numpy as np

# === CRITICAL: Split FIRST, then resample ===
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y, random_state=42
)

# === SMOTE (only on training data) ===
smote = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_train_res, y_train_res = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
print(f'Before SMOTE: {np.bincount(y_train)}')
print(f'After SMOTE:  {np.bincount(y_train_res)}')

# === Borderline-SMOTE (focus on boundary) ===
bsmote = BorderlineSMOTE(kind='borderline-1', random_state=42)
X_train_bs, y_train_bs = bsmote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# === ADASYN (adaptive to difficulty) ===
adasyn = ADASYN(random_state=42)
X_train_ada, y_train_ada = adasyn.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# === SMOTE + Tomek Links (clean boundaries) ===
smotetomek = SMOTETomek(random_state=42)
X_train_st, y_train_st = smotetomek.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# === Undersampling ===
rus = RandomUnderSampler(random_state=42)
X_train_rus, y_train_rus = rus.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# === Pipeline with resampling ===
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline as ImbPipeline
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

pipeline = ImbPipeline([
    ('sampling', SMOTE(random_state=42)),
    ('classifier', RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200))
])
pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)

sections.title: Cost-Sensitive Learning & Class Weights
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of changing the data distribution, change the loss function to penalize minority misclassifications more heavily. Every false negative for the rare class costs λ times more than a false positive.

Approaches:

  • Class Weights: Assign weight wj = n / (k · nj) to each sample of class j (inverse frequency). Common heuristic: balanced = n_samples / (n_classes × n_samples_in_class). Model minimizes weighted loss. Supported by most sklearn models.
  • Sample Weights: Assign individual weights per sample. More flexible — can up-weight specific minority examples (e.g., recent fraud cases more than old ones).
  • Cost Matrix: C(i,j) is the cost of predicting class i when true class is j. For fraud: C(not_fraud, fraud) = $100 (missed fraud), C(fraud, not_fraud) = $5 (false alarm). Train to minimize expected cost.

Cost-sensitive vs resampling:

  • Class weights preserve the original data distribution — no synthetic data, no information loss.
  • Weights are parameter-free (inverse frequency is a good default). SMOTE has hyperparameters (k neighbors, sampling strategy).
  • Class weights can be combined with SMOTE — SMOTE for diversity, class weights for emphasis.
  • Extreme imbalance (1000:1): class weights alone may not suffice. Combine with SMOTE or undersampling.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Fraud dataset with 0.1% fraud. Balanced class weight = 1/(2 × 0.001) = 500 for fraud class, 1/(2 × 0.999) ≈ 0.5 for non-fraud. Each fraud sample counts 1000x more than each non-fraud sample in the loss.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
import numpy as np

# === sklearn: class_weight ===
# 'balanced' = n_samples / (n_classes * n_samples_in_class)
rf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    class_weight='balanced',  # auto-compute from y
    random_state=42
)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Manual weights for more control
weights = {0: 1.0, 1: 10.0}  # minority class gets 10x weight
rf_custom = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    class_weight=weights
)

# === XGBoost: scale_pos_weight ===
xgb = XGBClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    scale_pos_weight=sum(y_train == 0) / sum(y_train == 1),  # ratio
    eval_metric='aucpr',
    random_state=42
)
xgb.fit(X_train, y_train)

# === SVM: class_weight ===
svm = SVC(kernel='rbf', class_weight='balanced', probability=True)
svm.fit(X_train, y_train)

# === Manual sample weights (any model) ===
# Up-weight recent samples
sample_weights = np.ones(len(y_train))
sample_weights[y_train == 1] = 10.0  # minority 10x
sample_weights[:100] *= 2.0  # recent samples get extra weight

rf_weighted = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200)
rf_weighted.fit(X_train, y_train, sample_weight=sample_weights)

# === Cost matrix example ===
# cost[i,j] = cost of predicting i when true is j
# For fraud: missing fraud costs $100, false alarm $5
cost_matrix = np.array([
    [0, 100],   # predict not fraud
    [5, 0]      # predict fraud
])

# Expected cost on test set
def expected_cost(y_true, y_pred_proba, cost_matrix):
    # For each sample, choose class that minimizes expected cost
    expected_cost_0 = y_pred_proba[:, 0] * cost_matrix[0,0] + \
                     y_pred_proba[:, 1] * cost_matrix[0,1]
    expected_cost_1 = y_pred_proba[:, 0] * cost_matrix[1,0] + \
                     y_pred_proba[:, 1] * cost_matrix[1,1]
    optimal_pred = expected_cost_1 < expected_cost_0
    total_cost = sum(cost_matrix[optimal_pred, y_true])
    return total_cost

sections.title: Threshold Moving
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: The default threshold of 0.5 assumes balanced classes and equal costs. For imbalanced data, the optimal threshold is almost never 0.5. Moving the threshold is often more effective than resampling.

Why threshold moving works:

  • The model's probability estimates are often well-calibrated even for imbalanced data. The problem isn't the probabilities — it's the decision threshold.
  • Threshold moving requires no data modification (no SMOTE, no class weights). Train on original data, then pick the right threshold.
  • You can tune the threshold to optimize any business metric (precision at 90% recall, max F1, cost minimization).

Finding the optimal threshold:

  • Maximize F1: Compute precision, recall, and F1 at every possible threshold. Pick the threshold that maximizes F1. This is the most common approach.
  • Youden's Index: J = sensitivity + specificity - 1. Maximizes the sum of TPR and TNR. Good when both classes matter equally.
  • Cost-based: Minimize total cost given the cost matrix. Choose threshold where expected cost is minimized on validation data.
  • Precision at target recall: Pick threshold that achieves a target recall (e.g., 90% recall for fraud detection). This is the most business-relevant approach.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Fraud detection model predicts probabilities. At threshold 0.5: precision = 80%, recall = 20%. At threshold 0.1: precision = 30%, recall = 85%. The business says “we can handle false alarms, catch more fraud” — use threshold 0.1 and get 4x more fraud caught.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve, f1_score
import numpy as np

# === Get predicted probabilities ===
y_pred_proba = model.predict_proba(X_val)[:, 1]

# === Find optimal threshold for max F1 ===
precisions, recalls, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y_val, y_pred_proba)

# Remove the last element (thresholds is 1 shorter)
f1_scores = 2 * (precisions[:-1] * recalls[:-1]) / (precisions[:-1] + recalls[:-1] + 1e-10)
best_idx = np.argmax(f1_scores)
best_threshold = thresholds[best_idx]
print(f'Best threshold for F1: {best_threshold:.3f} (F1={f1_scores[best_idx]:.3f})')

# === Evaluate at best threshold ===
y_pred_optimal = (y_pred_proba >= best_threshold).astype(int)
print(f'Optimal threshold F1: {f1_score(y_val, y_pred_optimal):.3f}')

# === Precision at target recall ===
def threshold_at_recall(y_true, y_scores, target_recall=0.9):
    precisions, recalls, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y_true, y_scores)
    idx = np.argmin(np.abs(recalls - target_recall))
    return thresholds[idx]

thresh_90 = threshold_at_recall(y_val, y_pred_proba, 0.9)
print(f'Threshold for 90% recall: {thresh_90:.3f}')

# === Youden's Index ===
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_val, y_pred_proba)
youden = tpr - fpr
best_y_idx = np.argmax(youden)
best_y_threshold = thresholds[best_y_idx]
print(f'Youden threshold: {best_y_threshold:.3f} (J={youden[best_y_idx]:.3f})')

# === Business cost optimization ===
def optimal_threshold_by_cost(y_true, y_scores, fn_cost, fp_cost):
    thresholds = np.sort(np.unique(y_scores))
    total_costs = []
    for t in thresholds:
        y_pred = (y_scores >= t).astype(int)
        fn = ((y_pred == 0) & (y_true == 1)).sum()
        fp = ((y_pred == 1) & (y_true == 0)).sum()
        total_costs.append(fn * fn_cost + fp * fp_cost)
    return thresholds[np.argmin(total_costs)]

# Missed fraud costs $100, false alarm costs $10
opt_t = optimal_threshold_by_cost(y_val, y_pred_proba, 100, 10)
print(f'Cost-optimal threshold: {opt_t:.3f}')

sections.title: Ensemble Methods for Imbalance
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Combine the power of ensembles with imbalance-specific sampling. Each base model sees a different balanced subset, and the ensemble averages their predictions for a robust final model.

Key methods:

  • Balanced Random Forest: Each tree in the forest is trained on a bootstrap sample where the minority class is oversampled or majority is undersampled to achieve balance. Works better than post-hoc SMOTE + RF in many cases.
  • EasyEnsemble: Create K balanced subsets by randomly undersampling the majority class (each subset = all minority + random majority). Train a classifier on each subset. Ensemble averages K classifiers. No information loss — every majority sample is used across some subset.
  • RUSBoost: Boosting variant that undersamples the majority class at each boosting iteration. Focuses the sequential training on different balanced views of the data. Often the best performing method for severe imbalance.
  • BalanceCascade: Similar to EasyEnsemble but removes correctly classified majority samples from subsequent subsets. Focuses on hard majority samples.
sections.codeBlock:
from imblearn.ensemble import (
    BalancedRandomForestClassifier,
    EasyEnsembleClassifier,
    RUSBoostClassifier
)
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, roc_auc_score
import numpy as np

# === Balanced Random Forest ===
brf = BalancedRandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    sampling_strategy='auto',  # balance each bootstrap
    replacement=True,
    random_state=42
)
brf.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_brf = brf.predict(X_test)
print(f'BRF F1: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_brf):.3f}')

# === EasyEnsemble ===
ee = EasyEnsembleClassifier(
    n_estimators=10,  # 10 subsets
    sampling_strategy='auto',
    random_state=42
)
ee.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_ee = ee.predict(X_test)
print(f'EasyEnsemble F1: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_ee):.3f}')

# === RUSBoost ===
rusb = RUSBoostClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    learning_rate=1.0,
    sampling_strategy='auto',
    random_state=42
)
rusb.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_rusb = rusb.predict(X_test)
print(f'RUSBoost F1: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_rusb):.3f}')

# === Compare all methods ===
results = {}
for name, model in [
    ('RF + class_weight', RandomForestClassifier(class_weight='balanced')),
    ('RF + SMOTE', ImbPipeline([('smote', SMOTE()), ('rf', RandomForestClassifier())])),
    ('Balanced RF', BalancedRandomForestClassifier()),
    ('EasyEnsemble', EasyEnsembleClassifier()),
    ('RUSBoost', RUSBoostClassifier()),
]:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
    results[name] = {
        'F1': f1_score(y_test, y_pred),
        'AUC-PR': roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)
    }
    print(f'{name:25s} F1={results[name]["F1"]:.3f} AUC-PR={results[name]["AUC-PR"]:.3f}')

sections.title: Evaluation Metrics for Imbalanced Data
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Accuracy lies. For imbalanced data, you need metrics that focus on the minority class. Precision-Recall curves are more informative than ROC curves when classes are imbalanced.

Why AUC-ROC is misleading:

  • ROC plots TPR vs FPR. FPR = FP / (FP + TN). When negatives dominate (99% majority), FPR is always small regardless of FP count. ROC can look excellent while the model is useless for the minority class.
  • AUC-PR uses precision = TP / (TP + FP) which directly measures minority class performance. Always prefer AUC-PR for imbalanced data.

Key metrics:

  • Precision: TP / (TP + FP). Of all positive predictions, how many were correct? Low = too many false alarms.
  • Recall (Sensitivity/TPR): TP / (TP + FN). Of all actual positives, how many did we catch? Low = missing too many minority cases.
  • F1 Score: Harmonic mean of precision and recall. Good single-number summary. But it assumes equal importance of precision and recall.
  • F-beta Score: Fβ = (1+β²) · (precision · recall) / (β² · precision + recall). β > 1 weights recall higher (catch more positives). β < 1 weights precision higher.
  • AUC-PR (Area Under PR Curve): Area under precision-recall curve. Baseline = positive class prevalence (if 1% minority, baseline = 0.01). AUC-PR near 0.01 is random, near 1.0 is perfect.
  • Lift Charts: Cumulative gains chart. Shows how much better the model is than random at each decile.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.metrics import (
    precision_recall_curve, average_precision_score, roc_auc_score,
    fbeta_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report
)
import numpy as np

# === Predict probabilities ===
y_pred_proba = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# === AUC-PR (preferred for imbalanced data) ===
auc_pr = average_precision_score(y_test, y_pred_proba)
baseline = y_test.mean()  # random classifier AUC-PR
print(f'AUC-PR: {auc_pr:.3f} (baseline={baseline:.3f})')

# === AUC-ROC (for reference) ===
auc_roc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_pred_proba)
print(f'AUC-ROC: {auc_roc:.3f}')  # often over-optimistic

# === F-beta (more recall weight) ===
# For fraud: catching fraud is critical, so beta=2 (recall 2x precision)
y_pred = (y_pred_proba >= 0.5).astype(int)
f2 = fbeta_score(y_test, y_pred, beta=2)
print(f'F2: {f2:.3f}')

# === PR Curve analysis ===
precisions, recalls, thresholds = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_pred_proba)

# Find precision at 90% recall
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(recalls - 0.9))
print(f'Precision at 90% recall: {precisions[idx]:.3f}')

# === Classification report ===
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

# === Lift at top decile ===
def lift_at_decile(y_true, y_scores, decile=0.1):
    n_top = int(len(y_true) * decile)
    top_idx = np.argsort(y_scores)[-n_top:]
    positive_in_top = y_true.iloc[top_idx].sum() if hasattr(y_true, 'iloc') else y_true[top_idx].sum()
    total_positive = y_true.sum()
    return (positive_in_top / n_top) / (total_positive / len(y_true))

print(f'Lift at top 10%: {lift_at_decile(y_test, y_pred_proba):.2f}x')

sections.title: Anomaly & Outlier Detection
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: When the minority class is extremely rare (< 0.1%) or has no labels, switch from classification to anomaly detection — model the “normal” distribution and flag anything that deviates.

Anomaly detection methods:

  • Isolation Forest: Builds random trees that isolate samples via random splits. Anomalies are isolated quickly (short path length in the tree). Normal points need many splits to isolate (long path). Fast, scalable, works well for high-dimensional data.
  • LOF (Local Outlier Factor): Measures local density deviation. Anomalies have much lower density than their neighbors. Works well for local anomalies (points that are anomalous relative to their region, not globally).
  • One-Class SVM: Learns a boundary around the normal data using a kernel. New points outside the boundary are anomalies. Good for medium-dimensional data. Sensitive to kernel choice and hyperparameters.
  • Autoencoder (Deep Learning): Train autoencoder to reconstruct normal data. Anomalies have high reconstruction error — they don't fit the learned “normal” manifold. Handles complex, high-dimensional data (images, sequences). Requires large amounts of normal data.

Novelty vs Outlier Detection:

  • Outlier detection: Training data contains both normal and anomalous points. The algorithm must identify which are which (unsupervised).
  • Novelty detection: Training data is clean (only normal). Detect new points that don't fit — these are “novel” anomalies.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from sklearn.neighbors import LocalOutlierFactor
from sklearn.svm import OneClassSVM
import numpy as np

# === Isolation Forest ===
iso_forest = IsolationForest(
    n_estimators=100,
    contamination=0.01,  # expected % of anomalies
    random_state=42
)
y_pred_if = iso_forest.fit_predict(X)
# 1 = normal, -1 = anomaly
anomalies_if = np.where(y_pred_if == -1)[0]
print(f'Isolation Forest detected {len(anomalies_if)} anomalies')

# === LOF (Local Outlier Factor) ===
lof = LocalOutlierFactor(
    n_neighbors=20,
    contamination=0.01
)
y_pred_lof = lof.fit_predict(X)
anomalies_lof = np.where(y_pred_lof == -1)[0]
print(f'LOF detected {len(anomalies_lof)} anomalies')

# === One-Class SVM (novelty detection) ===
# Train on clean data only
ocsvm = OneClassSVM(
    kernel='rbf',
    gamma='scale',
    nu=0.01  # upper bound on anomaly rate
)
ocsvm.fit(X_train_normal)
y_pred_ocsvm = ocsvm.predict(X_test)
# 1 = normal, -1 = anomaly

# === Autoencoder anomaly detection ===
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

def autoencoder_anomaly_detection(X_train, X_test, threshold_percentile=95):
    # Build a simple autoencoder
    from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
    
    # Use PCA as a linear autoencoder
    pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)  # 95% variance
    X_train_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_train)
    X_train_recon = pca.inverse_transform(X_train_pca)
    
    # Reconstruction error per sample
    train_errors = mean_squared_error(
        X_train, X_train_recon, multioutput='raw_values'
    )
    threshold = np.percentile(train_errors, threshold_percentile)
    
    # Find anomalies in test
    X_test_pca = pca.transform(X_test)
    X_test_recon = pca.inverse_transform(X_test_pca)
    test_errors = mean_squared_error(
        X_test, X_test_recon, multioutput='raw_values'
    )
    
    return test_errors > threshold

# === Evaluating anomaly detection (when labels exist) ===
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve, average_precision_score

# If you have ground truth labels
y_score = -iso_forest.decision_function(X_test)  # lower = more anomalous
ap = average_precision_score(y_test, y_score)
print(f'Anomaly detection AUC-PR: {ap:.3f}')
